Vision
To develop an ethical and innovative environment for the use and development of AI to accelerate Bangladesh’s path towards a middle-income country, as outlined in the Vision 2041 master plan and aligned with the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). 

Mission 
Establishing Bangladesh as a pioneer in AI innovation and adoption, creating a ‘Smart Bangladesh’ that leverages AI technologies for the well-being of all citizens, economic prosperity, and sustainable development in accord with four pillars; Smart Government, Smart Society, Smart Economy and Smart Citizen.

Objectives
-Ethical and secure AI governance and compliance
-Foster AI innovation for the public sector
-Promoting economic growth
-Strenghten AI readiness

Vision
Make provisions for processing personal data of a person for the purposes of legitimate use ensuring privacy, confidentiality and security and recognizing the personal data as personal right of a data-subject

Mission 
Creating a unified framework that aligns with international standards and ensures privacy, confidentiality, and security of personal data, recognizing it as a right of the data subject.

Objectives 
-Citizen focused control
-Consent based processes
-Digital sovereignty 
-Grievance redressal
-Responsible data fiduciary

Mission
Strengthen security through improved and focused legal frameworks

Objectives
-Defining roles and responsibilities of national cybersecurity institutions
-Establishing cyberemergency response protocols
-Imposing penalties for cyber offences and non-compliance
-Safeguarding Critical Information Infrastructure (CII)

Education:  The government plans on mandating the integration of AI education across secondary, tertiary, and technical institutions according to the Draft National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2026-30

Healthcare: National initiatives such as the Digital Health Strategy 2021–2026 and the Aspire to Innovate (a2i) program have already piloted e-health services in rural clinics. Private sector platforms like Jeeon, Praava Health, and Doctorola are expanding access to online consultations, prescriptions, and appointment scheduling for underserved populations.

Governance: 
Algorithmic Impact Assessments (AIAs) are implemented to monitor AI deployment to prevent biases and promote transparency in public administration.

Ekshop

Ekshop is a government platform that acts as an " e-commerce infrastructure backbone" for Bangladesh. A rural assisted e-commerce platform; it has brought together over 12 thousand rural artisans on one platform and provide a space for the sale of over 80 lakh products, as of 2023.

bKash

From 2021, bKash, a leading Mobile Financial Service (MFS) platform has introduced digital loans among other digital and financial services. bKash also saw a 24% increase in transactions and a 9% rise in its customer base in 2023.

MyBL

Nidle

Using IoT, Nidle allows workers and managers to monitor individual processes and performances, offering insights to optimize productivity and scaling while also decreasing wastage and rework. After its installation, there has been a 10% increase in productivity

__EMPTY_7:
National ID (Ongoing)

The National ID is a smart ID card containing an embedded microchip and featuring a unique 10-digit identification number – the National Identification Number (NIN). It is used to access public and private services such as banking, mobile registration and voter registration among other purposes. From 2011-2018, the World Bank Identification for Enhanced Access to Services (IDEAS) Project provided technical assistance. Due to implementation and policy delays, only 90 million NID cards (half the desired goal) was achieved by 2018
__EMPTY_8: Bangladesh NID Application System

__EMPTY_4: Digital Payments : Availability, Interoperability, financial inclusion
__EMPTY_7:
National Payment Switch (Ongoing)

Operational since 2012, the National Payment Switch Bangladesh (NPSB) is a platform that allows for seamless movement of funds from customers to banks to Mobile Financial Services (MFS) providers. It   supports extensive bank-to-wallet and MFS-to-MFS transactions,  with an aim to replace existing banking platforms with a unified, secure system
 Bank-to-MFS transfers fees set at Tk 1.5 per Tk 1,000; MFS-to-bank transfers set at Tk 8.5 per Tk 1,000; 2025

National E-Service Bus (Ongoing)

As of a document from August 2025, the National E-Service Bus was being used by around 15 government agencies including the NID agency, National Board of Revenue, BTRC, the Ministry of Agriculture, and the Office of the Registrar General. The platform still requires upgrades for scaling and broader adoption. The World Bank group is assisting with the software upgrading of the bus, along with standardized API specifications, operational processes, and technical capacity for government agencies to leverage the platform for service delivery

Dikkha (Digital Interactive Knack for Knowledgeable Human Assets)

A digital capacity building platform that focuses on providing students and teachers with technological support such as interactive classroom learning and help them upskill in an advanced technology like AI.

Doptor

A digital portal that provides adequate data on the entirety of Bangladesh and also provides a range of servies to all Bangladeshis and also individuals from outside of Bangladesh, making it accessible to everyone. It also aims on aiding the government and making e-service more efficient. Currently, it offers 10+ services and 40+ applications to about 14k offices and 140k users

Krishi Batayan; Agricultural Window

Under the department of Agricultural Extension of Bangladesh, Krishi Batayan; Agricultural Window is an integrated information and service portal that provides farmers with information on agricultural technology, crops, weather forecasts, insecticides and pesticides.

As of 2023, Only 44.5% of the population has access to the internet while 57.8 % of the population has an active mobile-broadband subscription. There exists a  gender gap of 13.2 % in internet access and 18.6 % in mobile phone access in 2022 as per Economist Impact

Payment infrastructure, although improved through mobile money services, still suffers from slow interbank settlements and inconsistent connectivity, especially in rural regions. Cyberattacks on financial institutions remain a major concern, with 52% of banks classified as high-risk in 2022 and an estimated 630 attempted intrusions through phishing scams and ponzi schemes daily.

-Bangladesh is a democratic republic with a unicameral parliament called the Jatiya Sangsad
-The president heads the state, currently President Abdul Hamid who is indirectly by the members of the parliament
- Out of the 300 members of parliament, 50 seats are reserved for women
The president appoints the head of the winning party as prime minister, currently Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina 
- The prime minister, advised by the president appoint members of the cabinet; currently 45 members

__EMPTY_7:
The Worldwide Governance Indicators analyse 6 areas of governance by relying on surveys and expert knowledge to assess the quality of governments with their potential to implement policy and also taking into consideration, the perception of citizens towards the performance of the state and existing institutions. The aggregates are statistically determined on an absolute 1-100 scale. They can be classified as follows:
-Voice and Accountability (The extent to which citizens can participate in the selection of government, their freedom of expression and holding officials accountable) : 36.53
-Political Stability (Measures political motivated violance and/or terrorism) : 40.90
-Government Effectiveness (Quality of public services) : 39.31
-Regulatory Quality (Ability to implement sound policy and laws) : 39.88
- Rule of Law (Quality of law enforcement) : 40.44 
-Control of Corruption (Captures perceptions of extent to which public power could be misused for private gain) : 25.55

Draft National Digital Transformation Strategy and ICT Reform Roadmap 2025 
VIsion
Transforming Bangladesh into a digitally empowered, innovative, and inclusive society through ICT-driven sustainable growth.

Mission
Drive digital transformation by strengthening governance, improving service delivery, and fostering economic development through strategic ICT initiatives

Objectives
-Empowering citizens
-Independent data and AI authority
-Massive AI driven upskilling and workforce transformation
-Strengthening cybersecurity and data governance frameworks

National Data Governance Ordinance, 2025
Vision
To set up a national data management authority to define policies, oversee compliance, address complaints, and maintain a National Source Code Repository, preventing vendor lock-in and ensuring accountability across data processors and custodians.

Objectives
-Establish data sovereignty
-Regulate data flow, especially cross border  
-Enforcing accountability and strict data rules
There is a rapid interest in building a phygital ecosystem but intiatives generally find themselves fragmented in nature due to the early stages of development

The AI policies and frameworks that could provide substantial grounding to many initiatives for the AI and DPI ecosystem still find themselves in draft stages, with data systems being siloed. This fragmentation further makes it difficult to effectively launch initiatives and/or sustain them long term

Unified Instant Payment System (Pipeline)

From September 2025, the Bangladesh bank aims to develop an interoperable payment system with the Gates foundation to allow for instant transfers  from mobile wallets, bank accounts, non-bank accounts, or institutional accounts directly to any other account. The Bank aims to establish a fully interoperable payment system by July 2027. The system will will be built on a Mojaloop-based IIPS and aim to link banks, mobile financial systems, insurance firms and other financial institutions.

-As of June 2024, the gender gap in internet use in Bangladesh stood at 0.767.
- 66.8 % of the urban population uses the internet versus only 29.7 % of the rural population. In urban areas, 71.3 % of men and 62.4 % of women use the internet, while in rural areas, 36.6 % of men and 23 % of women are reported users
-Internet usage in Bangladesh has reached about 47.4 per cent of the population but only 16 per cent of households have access to high-speed broadband internet
- According to the Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics ICT Usage Survey 2025, only 3.7 per cent of rural residents use a computer or laptop, compared to 20.7 per cent in urban areas. 
- While 50% or more of the population is covered by mobile network, many have not subscribed to services due to various socioeconomic barriers
- Senior citizens account for around 8 per cent of Bangladesh's population; many still struggle with digital interfaces in availing of banking, healthcare, and government services.
